---
title: "Corpus Change Detection Methods Zoo"
subtitle: "A comparative survey of structural break detection in the climate finance bibliography (1990–2024)"
author: "Minh Ha-Duong"
date: "2026-04-20"
metadata-files: [technical-report-vars.yml]
bibliography: bibliography/main.bib
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    number-sections: true
---

All scripts are in `scripts/`, the corpus contract files at `$CLIMATE_FINANCE_DATA/catalogs/`, and generated outputs in `content/figures/` and `content/tables/`.

# Overview

This document surveys the structural break detection methods applied to the climate finance corpus
({{< meta corpus_total >}} works, 1990--2024).
Each method answers the same question in a different vocabulary:
*does the distribution of works written before year $t$ differ from the distribution of works written after $t$?*
We apply a sliding window of $w$ years on each side of the candidate break year.^[Throughout this report we show $w \in \{2, 3, 4, 5\}$. The companion paper reports $w=3$ as the default lead window, with $w \in \{2, 4\}$ as robustness checks.]

Methods are grouped into three layers:

- **Part S — Semantic.** Compare full embedding distributions: each work is a point in the BAAI/bge-m3 embedding space ({{< meta emb_dimensions >}} dimensions). These methods detect distributional shift in *what the field talks about*.
- **Part L — Lexical.** Compare TF-IDF vocabulary distributions. Complementary to semantic methods; more interpretable (discriminating terms are directly readable).
- **Part G — Citation graph.** Compare citation network topology: degree distributions, community structure, centrality. These methods detect changes in *how knowledge flows* rather than *what is said*.

Classifier Two-Sample Tests (C2ST) appear as one genus in two habitats: C2ST\_embedding lives in Part S, C2ST\_lexical in Part L. Each asks the same meta-check question — can a machine-learning classifier tell "before" from "after" better than chance? — in its respective feature space.

## Standardisation: cross-year Z-scores

For each (method, window) pair we compute the time series $D(t, w)$ of divergence or distance values.
Within each window $w$, we standardise across years:

$$Z(t, w) = \frac{D(t,w) - \bar{D}(\cdot,w)}{\sigma_D(\cdot,w)}$$

This cross-year $Z$-score measures relative displacement from the temporal mean, not absolute magnitude.
It removes the long-run trend that dominates raw divergence values
and makes methods with different units comparable on the same panel.
A value $|Z| \geq 2$ indicates an unusually large (or small) divergence relative to the period average.

## Summary of findings

The 18 methods partition into four epistemic roles:

| Signal | Methods | Pattern |
|--------|---------|---------|
| **Field convergence** | S1–S4, L1, L2, G2, G9 | Peak Z ≈ +2–4 around 1998–2002; monotonic decline thereafter. Early climate finance was heterogeneous; the field homogenised over time. |
| **Structural break (topology)** | G5, G6 | Peak Z ≈ +2.4–3 at **2007**. Citation network topology — preferential attachment exponent and degree entropy — shifted sharply, consistent with the post-Stern/UNFCCC-Bali institutionalisation of climate finance. |
| **Reference / sanity check** | C2ST | Classifier AUC sits at 0.60–0.67 throughout — above chance in every year. Confirms that the distance-based methods are not measuring noise; does not vote on zones. |
| **Noisy / weak / cumulative** | G1, G3, G4, G7, G8, L3 | Weak or non-directional signal (G1 peak Z=1.3); L3 returns a term-level list rather than a scalar divergence. Useful as robustness checks and lexical fingerprints. |

\newpage

---


{{< include _includes/techrep-zoo.md >}}
